paragraphs.text: Having numbered the generations in the line of Cain and ended the account of Lamech’s words to his wives, Moses turns to the numbering of the generations in the line of Seth and, beginning with Adam, says: “And Adam lived a hundred and thirty years, and begat a son in his own likeness, after his image” (Gen 5:3). In Seth, who was in every way like Adam, is presented to us the likeness of the Son of God, who is the image of the Father who begot Him — as Seth is the image of Adam who begot him. When Enos was born to Seth, then, as Scripture says, “began men to call themselves by the name of the Lord” (Gen 4:26); that is, since Seth had separated himself from the line of Cain, his line began to be called by the name of the Lord, or the righteous people of the Lord.

Having said that Adam begat Seth, Seth begat Enos, Enos begat Cainan, Cainan begat Mahalaleel, Mahalaleel begat Jared, Jared begat Enoch, Scripture says: “And Enoch pleased God, and was not found” (Gen 5:24). Some interpret that Enoch was translated to paradise in Adam’s sight (before his eyes): both so that Adam might not think Enoch had been killed like Abel and grieve, and so that he might be comforted concerning his righteous son Abel and understand that all who are like him are taken up to paradise either before death or after the resurrection.

Enoch begat Methuselah, Methuselah begat Lamech, Lamech begat Noah. And Lamech prophesied concerning his son, saying: “This same shall give us rest by his sacrifice, by which he shall appease God for our works and for the toil of our hands, and for the ground which the Lord God hath cursed” (Gen 5:29), because for the sins of those who lived on the earth God will destroy in the waters of wrath the houses and gardens we built, over which our hands have labored.

After numbering ten generations from Adam to Noah, Moses says of Noah that “he was five hundred years old, and Noah begat three sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth” (Gen 5:32). Noah, who kept virginity for so long a time, served as a model for his contemporaries; for he kept virginity for five hundred years among men of whom it is said, “all flesh had corrupted its way” (Gen 6:12).
